name: Spinal Fusion (Post-Op — Lumbar)

phases.weeks_0_6: Breathing + ankle pumps + walking + gentle ROM. Strict BLT precautions (no Bending, Lifting, Twisting).
phases.weeks_6_12: Add isometrics + bodyweight core + glute activation. Surgeon clearance required.
phases.weeks_12_24: Progressive loading with fusion-safe exercises. Neutral spine mandatory.
phases.months_6_plus: Surgeon-specific progression toward broader loading with neutral spine control. Adjacent segment mobility work.

referOutIf: New radiating pain, progressive numbness/weakness in legs, bowel/bladder changes, hardware pain, fever, wound changes
userMessage: Your fusion means we protect the fused segments permanently. Core training is anti-movement only — we build a natural brace around the fusion. Adjacent segments need mobility work to prevent degeneration above/below the fusion.
notes: Staged progression replaces lifetime blanket ban. Early: strict BLT. Later: surgeon-guided progression toward loading WITH neutral control. Adjacent segment disease prevention is priority.
source: Spine surgery rehabilitation protocols, NASM CPT special populations

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Spinal Fusion (Post-Op — Cervical)

phases.weeks_0_6: Collar protocol per surgeon, gentle hand/wrist exercises only
phases.weeks_6_12: Isometric neck, scapular stabilization, gentle ROM
phases.months_6_plus: Return to most activities with permanent loaded cervical motion restriction

referOutIf: New radiating arm pain, progressive numbness/weakness, difficulty swallowing, voice changes, myelopathy signs (gait changes, hand clumsiness)
userMessage: Cervical fusion requires permanent protection of the fused levels. We focus on scapular stability and upper body strength while avoiding loaded neck movements.
notes: Similar adjacent segment concerns as lumbar fusion. Protect fused levels permanently.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Disc Herniation (Lumbar — Acute)
referOutIf: Bowel/bladder changes (cauda equina emergency), saddle numbness, bilateral leg weakness, progressive neurological deficit, symptoms peripheralize with ALL directions
userMessage: Acute disc herniation is routed through directional preference testing FIRST. Extension helps most (but not all) disc herniations. If symptoms peripheralize with extension, we switch to neutral-only or flexion-based protocol.
notes: Do NOT hard-code extension. Route through directional preference testing. Some patients centralize with extension, others worsen. Cauda equina syndrome is a surgical emergency.
source: McKenzie R. Treat Your Own Back, StatPearls NBK539720

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Disc Herniation (Lumbar — Chronic/Managed)
referOutIf: New radiating symptoms, increased frequency of flare-ups, progressive weakness
userMessage: Your managed disc requires daily McKenzie press-ups and McGill Big 3. Core endurance (not max strength) is the priority.
notes: Maintenance phase. Daily McKenzie press-ups. Core endurance (not strength) is priority per McGill research.
source: McKenzie MDT, McGill S. Low Back Disorders

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Disc Herniation (Cervical)
referOutIf: Symptoms peripheralize, progressive arm weakness, myelopathy signs (gait changes, hand clumsiness)
userMessage: Cervical disc issues respond to retraction-based exercises. We start with chin tucks and progress through the McKenzie neck protocol.
notes: Same centralization principle as lumbar. Retraction is the foundation movement.
source: McKenzie R. Treat Your Own Neck

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Spinal Stenosis
referOutIf: Neurogenic claudication worsening, bowel/bladder changes, progressive weakness
userMessage: Stenosis responds to flexion-based exercises — the OPPOSITE of disc herniation. Biking and swimming are often better tolerated than walking.
notes: FLEXION-biased protocol — OPPOSITE of disc herniation. Extension narrows the spinal canal.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Spondylolisthesis
avoid: All jumping activities (both feet leaving ground)
referOutIf: Worsening leg symptoms, progressive slip on imaging, new neurological signs
userMessage: Forward slip of the vertebra is worsened by extension. We focus on flexion-biased exercises and aggressive core stabilization.
notes: Forward slip worsened by extension. Flexion-biased + aggressive core stabilization.
source: NASM CPT Special Populations, Spine rehabilitation protocols

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Spondylolysis (Pars Fracture)
referOutIf: Persistent pain despite rest, neurological symptoms, pain with daily activities
userMessage: Stress fracture of the pars requires rest from extension and rotation. Core stabilization protects the healing bone.
notes: Stress fracture of pars interarticularis. Extension and rotation stress the fracture.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Degenerative Disc Disease
avoid: Conventional deadlifts and heavy barbell back squats
Clean and jerk, snatch (explosive spinal loading)
referOutIf: New radiating symptoms, progressive weakness, significant functional decline
userMessage: Degenerative discs benefit from movement — but we protect them under load. Your directional preference (extension vs flexion) guides exercise selection.
notes: Depends on presentation — may be extension or flexion biased. Movement is medicine but protect under load.
source: McGill S. Low Back Disorders, ACSM Guidelines

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Chronic Neck Pain (Non-Specific)
referOutIf: Radiating arm symptoms, progressive weakness, headaches increasing in frequency
userMessage: Most chronic neck pain comes from upper crossed syndrome. We strengthen deep neck flexors and lower traps while stretching upper traps and pecs.
notes: Usually upper crossed syndrome. Strengthen deep cervical flexors, lower traps. Stretch upper traps, pecs. Thoracic mobility is critical.
source: Janda Upper Crossed Syndrome, McKenzie MDT

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Whiplash (Chronic)
referOutIf: Worsening symptoms, new neurological signs, dizziness/vertigo
userMessage: Chronic whiplash responds to graded exposure to movement. We start very gently and progress based on your tolerance.
notes: Graded exposure to movement. Pain science education important. May have central sensitization component.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Sciatica
referOutIf: Bowel/bladder changes, saddle numbness, bilateral symptoms, progressive weakness, no improvement after 6 weeks of appropriate treatment
userMessage: Sciatica has three distinct causes requiring different treatments. We classify yours first: disc-related (extension may help), stenosis-related (flexion helps), or piriformis-related (stretching/release helps). Don't mix strategies.
notes: MUST classify subtype before prescribing. Disc-related: extension bias. Stenosis-related: flexion bias. Piriformis: stretching + release. Mixing strategies is ineffective and potentially harmful.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: SI Joint Dysfunction
referOutIf: Persistent pain despite stabilization, leg length discrepancy noted, sacral fracture suspected
userMessage: SI joint dysfunction responds to core and hip stabilization. We build symmetrical strength around the pelvis.
notes: Stabilize the joint. Often coexists with hip weakness. May need SI belt initially.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Ankylosing Spondylitis
referOutIf: Progressive stiffness despite exercise, new joint involvement, eye inflammation (uveitis)
userMessage: Extension and posture exercises are KEY to prevent progressive kyphosis. Swimming is the gold standard. Chest expansion exercises maintain respiratory function.
notes: Extension + posture prevents progressive kyphosis. Swimming is gold standard.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Kyphosis (Excessive Thoracic)
referOutIf: Scheuermann's disease suspected (adolescent), progressive deformity, neurological symptoms
userMessage: We use a 2:1 pull-to-push ratio, daily thoracic extension, and strengthen your mid/lower traps while stretching your pecs.
notes: 2:1 pull-to-push ratio minimum. Thoracic extension mobility daily.
source: NASM CPT Corrective Exercise, Janda Upper Crossed Syndrome

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Lordosis (Excessive Lumbar)
referOutIf: Neurological symptoms, structural lordosis (vs postural)
userMessage: Lower crossed syndrome — we lengthen your hip flexors and activate your glutes and deep core to restore neutral pelvis.
notes: Lower crossed syndrome. Lengthen hip flexors + erectors. Activate glutes + TVA.
source: Janda Lower Crossed Syndrome, NASM CPT

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Scoliosis
referOutIf: Progressive curve on imaging, respiratory compromise, pain worsening
userMessage: Scoliosis training is more nuanced than 'strengthen one side.' We focus on spinal stabilization, breathing mechanics, and functional symmetry based on YOUR curve pattern.
notes: Add curve type (C vs S), magnitude (mild <25, moderate 25-45, severe >45), and skeletal maturity. 'Strengthen the convex side' alone is oversimplified. Focus on: spinal stabilization, bilateral symmetry, breathing mechanics, and functional movement. Schroth method is evidence-based.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Coccyx Pain (Coccydynia)
referOutIf: Persistent pain >6 weeks, suspected fracture, infection signs
userMessage: We avoid direct coccyx pressure. Standing or supine exercises preferred.
notes: Avoid direct coccyx pressure. Standing or supine exercises preferred.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Failed Back Surgery Syndrome (FBSS)
avoid: Determined by individual assessment — varies widely
referOutIf: New neurological deficits, worsening pain pattern, psychological decompensation
userMessage: Complex chronic pain requires a careful, graded approach. Exercise is medicine but must be dosed carefully. We start very low and progress based on your response.
notes: Complex chronic pain condition. Biopsychosocial approach essential. May have central sensitization.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Thoracic Outlet Syndrome
referOutIf: Arm/hand color changes (blue, white, mottled), significant swelling, cold hand, absent pulse, throbbing pain — these suggest vascular TOS requiring emergency evaluation
userMessage: We open the thoracic outlet with stretching and posture work — stretching scalenes and pec minor while correcting forward head posture.
notes: Must subtype: neurogenic (most common, nerve compression), venous (vein compression), arterial (artery compression — vascular emergency). Neurogenic: posture correction + scalene stretching. Venous/arterial: IMMEDIATE referral.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

venous.description: Vein compression. Refer to vascular specialist.

name: Knee — ACL Tear (Post-Op)

phases.phase1: ROM restoration, quad activation, gait normalization. Criteria: full extension, flexion >120deg, no effusion.
phases.phase2: Strengthening (OKC/CKC). Criteria: quad strength >70% uninvolved, single leg squat without deviation.
phases.phase3: Running progression. Criteria: quad >80%, hop tests >80%, no pain/effusion with plyometrics.
phases.phase4: Sport-specific + return. Criteria: quad >=90%, hop tests >=90% symmetry, passes sport-specific testing.

referOutIf: New instability episodes, significant effusion, loss of ROM >10 degrees, graft site pain worsening, mechanical locking
userMessage: ACL rehab is a 9-12 month process. We prioritize quad activation, VMO strengthening, and progressive loading with strict phase gating.
notes: Replace month-based return with criteria-based. Pivoting when: quad strength >=90% uninvolved, hop test >=90% symmetry, no effusion. Graft type (BTB vs hamstring vs quad vs allograft) affects timeline and donor site precautions.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Knee — Meniscus Tear/Repair
referOutIf: Mechanical locking, giving way, significant effusion, inability to bear weight, loss of ROM
userMessage: Meniscus work means limiting deep flexion under load. We strengthen quads and VMO while respecting ROM limits.
notes: Three distinct protocols: (1) Nonoperative: load management + quad strengthening. (2) Surgical repair: weight-bearing restrictions + limited ROM early. (3) Root/ramp repair: extended protection, slower progression. Protocol depends on tear type, location, and vascularity.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

nonoperative.description: Load management + quad strengthening. May allow full activity if asymptomatic.
nonoperative.restrictions: Avoid deep squat if symptomatic. Monitor for mechanical symptoms.

surgical_repair.description: Weight-bearing restrictions early. ROM limited per surgeon. Slower return.
surgical_repair.restrictions: No deep flexion 6-8 weeks. Progressive loading after 12 weeks.

root_ramp_repair.description: Extended protection. Slower progression than standard repair.
root_ramp_repair.restrictions: Partial WB 4-6 weeks. No deep squat 12+ weeks. Full return 6-9 months.

name: Knee — Patellar Tendinopathy
referOutIf: Complete inability to load the tendon, significant swelling, night pain, pain at rest worsening
userMessage: Tendon loading is the treatment — not rest. We use the 24-hour pain rule: if pain increases >2/10 and lasts >24 hours after exercise, we reduce load. Gradual jump return is allowed as symptoms permit.
notes: Use tendon-loading progression, NOT VMO-specific training. Pain monitoring: if pain increases >2/10 AND lasts >24 hours post-exercise, reduce load (24-hour pain rule). Allow graded jump return — blanket avoidance delays recovery.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Knee — Osteoarthritis
referOutIf: Sudden worsening, joint deformity, inability to weight-bear
userMessage: Movement is medicine for OA. Quad strengthening is the biggest protective factor. Low-impact cardio keeps joints healthy.
notes: Strengthen quads (biggest protective factor). Heat before, ice after.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Knee — Total Replacement (Post-Op)

phases.months_3_plus: Return to most activities except high impact

referOutIf: Signs of infection, sudden loss of ROM, persistent warmth/redness, DVT symptoms
userMessage: Knee replacement rehab focuses on ROM recovery first, then progressive strengthening. High impact is permanently avoided.
notes: High impact permanently restricted. ROM recovery is priority.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Shoulder — Rotator Cuff Tear/Repair
referOutIf: Complete tear suspected, progressive weakness, night pain worsening
userMessage: Rotator cuff rehab starts with pendulum movements and isometrics, progressing to band work and scapular exercises as healing allows.
notes: Follow surgeon/PT protocol strictly. Progression is tissue-healing dependent.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Shoulder — Labrum Tear (SLAP/Bankart)
referOutIf: Recurrent instability, new catching/locking, significant strength loss, inability to perform ADLs
userMessage: Your labrum tear means we build rotator cuff strength before any pressing, use landmine press as our safe pressing option, and never go behind the neck or to end-range overhead.
notes: SLAP and Bankart lesions require separate protocols. SLAP: biceps loading restrictions, overhead return depends on repair type. Bankart: instability precautions, avoid positions of apprehension (ER + abduction + extension).

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

slap.description: Superior labrum tear. Biceps anchor involved.
slap.restrictions: Limit biceps loading early. Overhead return gradual. Type II repair most restrictive.

bankart.restrictions: Avoid apprehension position. No behind-neck pressing. Gradual ER in abduction.

name: Shoulder — Frozen Shoulder (Adhesive Capsulitis)
referOutIf: No improvement after 3 months, loss of function affecting daily life
userMessage: Frozen shoulder requires gentle, persistent ROM work. Pendulum exercises and gradual stretching — never force through the adhesions.
notes: Gentle progressive ROM. Do not force through adhesions. Condition self-limits in 1-3 years.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Shoulder — Impingement Syndrome
avoid: Lateral raises above 90° with internal rotation
Bench press without scapular control (use caution)
referOutIf: Persistent pain >6 weeks despite corrective program, night pain, weakness progression
userMessage: Impingement responds to scapular stabilization, rotator cuff strengthening, thoracic extension, and pec stretching.
notes: Scapular control is key. Open subacromial space with posture correction.
source: NASM CPT Corrective Exercise, Shoulder impingement protocols

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Shoulder — Total Replacement
referOutIf: Dislocation signs, infection signs, progressive loss of ROM
userMessage: Shoulder replacement requires permanent activity modifications. We focus on gentle ROM and scapular stability.
notes: Follow surgeon protocol strictly. Permanent restrictions likely.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Hip — Total Replacement (Posterior Approach)
referOutIf: Dislocation signs (shortening, rotation), infection signs, DVT symptoms
userMessage: Posterior approach precautions: no flexion past 90°, no crossing legs, no internal rotation for 6-12 weeks.
notes: No combined flexion + adduction + internal rotation.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Hip — Total Replacement (Anterior Approach)
referOutIf: Same as posterior approach
userMessage: Anterior approach has fewer restrictions than posterior. Faster return to function typically.
notes: Fewer precautions than posterior approach.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Hip — Labral Tear
avoid: Hip flexion with adduction and internal rotation
Prolonged sitting in deep hip flexion
referOutIf: Persistent catching/locking, progressive pain, functional limitation
userMessage: Hip labral tears require glute strengthening and avoiding deep flexion positions that compress the labrum.
notes: Avoid positions that compress labrum. Strengthen glutes.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Hip — Bursitis (Trochanteric)
referOutIf: No improvement after 6 weeks, signs of infection, severe swelling
userMessage: Hip bursitis (GTPS) responds to gluteal strengthening and avoiding compression. We've removed IT band foam rolling — research shows it can aggravate this condition.
notes: Remove IT band foam rolling — it's often too provocative for greater trochanteric pain syndrome (GTPS). Focus on gluteal loading (clamshells, side-lying abduction) and avoiding compression positions (crossing legs, side-lying on affected side, standing on one leg).

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Hip — Osteoarthritis
referOutIf: Sudden worsening, inability to weight-bear, joint deformity
userMessage: Hip OA benefits from low-impact movement. Aquatic exercise and cycling are excellent options.
notes: Movement is medicine. Low-impact preferred.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Ankle — Chronic Instability
referOutIf: Recurrent sprains, mechanical instability, persistent swelling
userMessage: Chronic ankle instability responds to proprioception training and peroneal strengthening.
notes: Proprioception training is key. Progressive unstable surfaces.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Ankle — Post-Fracture
referOutIf: Pain at fracture site, new deformity, inability to weight-bear after clearance
userMessage: Post-fracture rehab starts with gentle ROM and progresses to strengthening and balance work as bone healing confirms.
notes: Weight-bearing per orthopedic protocol. Progress ROM based on healing.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Elbow — Tennis Elbow (Lateral Epicondylitis)
referOutIf: No improvement after 6 weeks of eccentric program, weakness progression
userMessage: Tennis elbow responds to eccentric wrist extension (Tyler twist). We progressively load the tendon.
notes: Eccentric loading is gold standard. Tyler twist protocol.
source: Tyler twist protocol, Alfredson eccentric principles

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Elbow — Golfer's Elbow (Medial Epicondylitis)
referOutIf: No improvement after 6 weeks, ulnar nerve symptoms
userMessage: Golfer's elbow treatment mirrors tennis elbow but targets the flexor side. Reduce grip demand in main lifts.
notes: Flexor-side tendinopathy. Reduce grip demand in main lifts.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Wrist — Carpal Tunnel Syndrome
avoid: Push-ups on flat hands (extreme wrist extension under load)
Planks on flat hands (use fists or wrist wraps)
Heavy gripping without wrist straps (deadlifts, pull-ups)
Hyperextension or hyperflexion of wrist during any exercise
referOutIf: Thenar muscle wasting, progressive numbness, dropping objects, failed conservative treatment
userMessage: Nerve glides and wrist neutral positioning during exercises. Avoid positions that compress the carpal tunnel.
notes: Nerve glides + wrist neutral positioning during exercises.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Wrist — De Quervain's Tenosynovitis

phases.acute: Rest, ice, thumb-spica brace, activity modification. Avoid gripping/pinching.
phases.subacute: Gentle isometric wrist exercises. Continue bracing during aggravating activities.
phases.chronic: Progressive loading. Eccentric wrist exercises if non-irritable. Grip strengthening.

referOutIf: No improvement with rest and rehab, severe functional limitation
userMessage: De Quervain's requires avoiding thumb-stressing grip patterns and gentle stretching of the affected tendons.
notes: Activity modification and thumb-spica protection FIRST, not early eccentric loading for irritable cases. Isometric wrist loading after acute phase resolves. Early eccentrics can worsen irritable tendons.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Foot — Plantar Fasciitis
avoid: Running on hard surfaces during acute phase
High-impact aerobics (jumping jacks, box jumps) during acute phase
referOutIf: No improvement after 3 months, severe first-step pain worsening, suspected rupture
userMessage: Plantar fasciitis treatment includes calf work, foot intrinsic strengthening, specific stretches, and arch support. We address the whole kinetic chain, not just the calf.
notes: Calf eccentrics alone are incomplete. Add: plantar fascia-specific stretching (towel curls, toe yoga), intrinsic foot strengthening, taping for acute relief, and ankle/foot strengthening (tibialis raises).

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Foot — Achilles Tendinopathy
referOutIf: Complete inability to push off, palpable gap in tendon (rupture concern), sudden pop with pain
userMessage: Achilles tendinopathy responds to the Alfredson eccentric protocol — slow, heavy eccentrics off a step.
notes: MUST split midportion from insertional. Alfredson eccentric drops BELOW neutral can aggravate insertional cases. Midportion: eccentric drops off step OK. Insertional: eccentric drops only to neutral (not below), add isometric loading first.
source: Alfredson H. Heavy-load eccentric calf muscle training

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

midportion.description: 2-6cm above insertion. Classic Alfredson protocol appropriate.
midportion.restrictions: Standard eccentric drop off step edge is safe.

insertional.description: At calcaneus attachment. Different protocol needed.
insertional.restrictions: NO eccentric drops below neutral — aggravates insertion. Isometric loading first, flat-ground eccentrics only.

name: Jaw — TMJ Disorder
avoid: Exercises causing jaw clenching — cue relaxed jaw during ALL exercises

modifications.cueJawRelaxation: tongue on roof, teeth apart, lips together

referOutIf: Locked jaw, progressive limited opening, severe pain with eating
userMessage: Jaw tension often increases during heavy exertion. We cue jaw relaxation during ALL exercises.
notes: Cue: tongue on roof of mouth, teeth apart, lips together during all exercises.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Hand — Trigger Finger / Dupuytren's
referOutIf: Locked finger, progressive contracture, failure of conservative treatment
userMessage: Trigger finger needs gentle tendon gliding, NOT grip strengthening. Heavy gripping makes it worse.
notes: Grip strengthening can WORSEN trigger finger. Focus on gentle ROM, tendon gliding exercises, and activity modification. Separate from Dupuytren's — different pathology, different treatment.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Rheumatoid Arthritis
referOutIf: New joint involvement, severe flare unresponsive to medication, joint deformity
userMessage: Exercise between flares. Protect inflamed joints during flares (gentle ROM only). Resistance training does NOT worsen joint damage.
notes: Exercise between flares. Resistance training shown to NOT worsen joint damage.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Psoriatic Arthritis
avoid: Same as RA — high impact during flares, exercising inflamed joints
referOutIf: Same as RA triggers
userMessage: Similar to RA management. May also have skin considerations for equipment contact.
notes: Similar to RA. Equipment contact may need padding for skin involvement.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Gout
avoid: Impact on affected joint during flare
referOutIf: Recurrent flares despite medication, tophi formation, joint damage
userMessage: NO exercise during acute gout flare. Between flares, exercise normally. Stay hydrated.
notes: NO exercise during acute flare. Between flares, exercise normally.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Osteoporosis / Osteopenia
referOutIf: New fracture with minimal trauma, height loss, progressive deformity
userMessage: Weight-bearing exercise and resistance training load bones progressively. Balance training prevents falls, which is critical for fracture prevention.
notes: Load bones progressively. Falls prevention through balance training is critical.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Lupus (SLE)
referOutIf: Severe flare, renal involvement, new organ symptoms
userMessage: Short exercise bouts with fatigue management. Avoid photosensitivity triggers if exercising outdoors.
notes: Fatigue management critical. Avoid sun exposure triggers.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Fibromyalgia
referOutIf: Worsening despite graded approach, suspected other diagnosis, severe functional decline
userMessage: START EXTREMELY LOW. 5 min sessions. Build 10% per week max. Warm water exercise is gold standard. Pain ≠ tissue damage here.
notes: Central sensitization — pain ≠ tissue damage. Start 5 min, build 10%/week.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Chronic Fatigue Syndrome (ME/CFS)
avoid: Graded exercise therapy if causes PEM
referOutIf: Post-exertional malaise lasting >72 hours, orthostatic intolerance worsening, cognitive decline
userMessage: CRITICAL: Post-exertional malaise (PEM) is the defining feature. Exercise must stay BELOW your energy envelope. This is NOT deconditioning — do not push.
notes: NOT deconditioning. PEM is pathological. Stay below energy envelope.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Ehlers-Danlos Syndrome (Hypermobility Type)
referOutIf: Joint subluxation/dislocation, vascular EDS symptoms, autonomic dysfunction worsening
userMessage: Strengthen, don't stretch. Your joints are already too mobile. Focus on stability and proprioception.
notes: Strengthen, don't stretch. Joints already too mobile. Proprioception training.
source: EDS exercise guidelines, Mulvey et al.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Polymyalgia Rheumatica
referOutIf: GCA symptoms (headache, vision changes), steroid side effects worsening
userMessage: Often on corticosteroids — include weight-bearing exercise for bone protection. Gentle ROM and walking are your foundation.
notes: Often on steroids — bone density concerns. Include weight-bearing.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Sjögren's Syndrome
referOutIf: Lymphoma screening triggers, severe organ involvement
userMessage: Stay hydrated. Joint pain management similar to RA. Fatigue management is key.
notes: Stay hydrated. Joint pain similar to RA. Fatigue management.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Myasthenia Gravis
referOutIf: Respiratory muscle weakness, myasthenic crisis, bulbar symptoms worsening
userMessage: Muscle fatigue worsens with use. Short bouts with rest. Avoid overheating.
notes: Muscle fatigue worsens with use. Short bouts. Avoid overheating.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Polymyositis / Dermatomyositis
referOutIf: CK levels rising, new rash, respiratory muscle weakness
userMessage: During active disease: gentle ROM only. During remission: progressive resistance rebuilds lost strength.
notes: Inflammatory muscle disease. Exercise during remission builds back lost strength.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Raynaud's Disease
avoid: Outdoor exercise in cold without protection
referOutIf: Digital ulcers, severe tissue damage, secondary Raynaud's workup needed
userMessage: Exercise improves circulation. Avoid cold triggers. Warm hands before gripping weights.
notes: Exercise improves circulation. Avoid cold triggers.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Complex Regional Pain Syndrome (CRPS)
referOutIf: Spreading symptoms, severe functional loss, psychological decompensation
userMessage: Specialized pain condition. Movement is essential but must be graded extremely carefully. Pain science education is critical.
notes: Graded motor imagery and mirror therapy. Movement graded extremely carefully.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Parkinson's Disease
referOutIf: Falls increasing, freezing episodes worsening, medication timing changes affecting exercise
userMessage: Amplitude-based training (LSVT BIG). Boxing is excellent. Exercise may slow progression. Falls prevention is critical.
notes: Boxing excellent. Exercise may slow progression. Falls prevention critical.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Multiple Sclerosis
referOutIf: New relapse symptoms, worsening spasticity, severe fatigue pattern change
userMessage: Exercise in cool environments. Short bouts. Benefits: strength, balance, spasticity reduction.
notes: Cool environments. Short bouts. Fatigue management.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Stroke (Post-CVA)
referOutIf: New neurological symptoms (sudden weakness, vision changes, severe headache, speech difficulty), falls, shoulder subluxation signs, significant BP changes during exercise
userMessage: Neuroplasticity — repetition is key. Include affected side in every session.
notes: Add shoulder protection (subluxation risk on affected side), fall risk screening, cognition screening, and explicit referral triggers. Affected-side shoulder support during all activities.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Spinal Cord Injury (Incomplete)
referOutIf: Autonomic dysreflexia signs (sudden BP spike >20mmHg, pounding headache, flushing, sweating above level) — THIS IS AN EMERGENCY. Also: new pressure sores, new neurological changes, fracture signs, UTI symptoms
userMessage: Programming depends entirely on level and completeness. Specialist guidance essential.
notes: Add pressure-relief (weight shifts every 15-30 min), skin checks (before/after exercise), autonomic dysreflexia triggers (level T6 and above — EMERGENCY if BP rises >20mmHg with headache/flushing), orthostatic precautions (sit up slowly), and fracture risk (osteoporotic bones below level of injury).

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Peripheral Neuropathy
avoid: Exercises with fall risk without support
referOutIf: Rapid progression, new onset, severe balance impairment
userMessage: Falls prevention is paramount. Supervised balance training in a safe environment. Check feet daily.
notes: Falls prevention paramount. Supervised balance training.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Traumatic Brain Injury (Post-Acute)
referOutIf: Symptom exacerbation with minimal activity, new neurological signs, seizures
userMessage: Exercise below symptom exacerbation threshold. Gradually increase. Monitor for headache, dizziness, cognitive fog.
notes: Sub-symptom threshold exercise. Monitor for headache, dizziness, cognitive fog.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Cerebral Palsy
referOutIf: New contractures, functional decline, pain increase
userMessage: Highly individualized. Strengthening does NOT increase spasticity. Include flexibility for tight muscles.
notes: Strengthening does NOT increase spasticity (myth disproven).

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Guillain-Barré Syndrome (Recovery)
referOutIf: Relapse signs, respiratory muscle weakness, new weakness
userMessage: Recovery can take months to years. Progressive but must respect fatigue limits.
notes: Progressive but respect fatigue limits. Recovery months-years.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Essential Tremor
referOutIf: Tremor suddenly worsening, new neurological signs
userMessage: Exercise doesn't worsen tremor. Weight training may actually reduce tremor temporarily.
notes: Weight training may reduce tremor temporarily.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Vertigo / Vestibular Dysfunction
referOutIf: Central cause suspected, progressive hearing loss, persistent vertigo despite rehab
userMessage: Vestibular rehabilitation exercises and gaze stabilization. Epley maneuver for BPPV.
notes: Epley maneuver for BPPV. Gaze stabilization for vestibular hypofunction.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Epilepsy
referOutIf: Seizure frequency increasing with exercise, new seizure type
userMessage: Exercise generally REDUCES seizure frequency. Safety precautions for activities where seizure could be dangerous.
notes: Exercise generally reduces seizure frequency. Safety precautions for dangerous activities.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Migraine
referOutIf: New-onset headache pattern, aura changes, neurological symptoms
userMessage: Regular moderate aerobic exercise reduces migraine frequency. Avoid triggers: dehydration, overheating, skipping meals.
notes: Regular aerobic exercise shown to reduce migraine frequency.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: COPD
referOutIf: SpO2 dropping significantly, acute exacerbation, increasing dyspnea at rest
userMessage: Breathing technique integrated into every exercise. Interval training may be better tolerated than continuous.
notes: Integrate breathing with every exercise. Interval training preferred.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Heart Failure (Stable/Compensated)
referOutIf: Systolic BP drops >20mmHg during exercise, HR >120 or new irregular rhythm, SpO2 <88%, new/worsening dyspnea at rest, weight gain >3lbs overnight (fluid retention), new lower extremity edema
userMessage: Medical clearance required. Exercise improves function and quality of life. Monitor symptoms closely.
notes: Vitals and symptom thresholds: STOP if systolic BP drops >20mmHg, HR >120, SpO2 <88%, or new/worsening dyspnea at rest. Start with interval training (exercise 2-6 min, rest 1-2 min). RPE-based intensity (HR unreliable if on beta-blockers).

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Post-Cardiac Event (MI, CABG, Stent)
referOutIf: Chest pain/pressure, severe dyspnea, dizziness/syncope, heart rate >120 or irregular rhythm, BP >180/110 or drop >20mmHg systolic, SpO2 <90%
userMessage: Cardiac rehab Phase I-IV protocol. Heart rate monitoring essential. Medical clearance before independent exercise.
notes: Add cardiac rehab phases with vitals thresholds and symptom monitoring. Phase I (inpatient), Phase II (supervised outpatient), Phase III (independent). Sternotomy precautions if CABG: no pushing/pulling/lifting >5lbs for 8-12 weeks.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Hypertension
referOutIf: BP >180/120 before exercise, symptoms during exercise, medication not controlling BP
userMessage: Exercise lowers blood pressure. Avoid breath-holding. Monitor BP before exercise if uncontrolled.
notes: Exercise lowers BP. Avoid breath-holding.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Asthma (Exercise-Induced)
avoid: Cold dry air exercise without management
referOutIf: Bronchospasm not responding to inhaler, increasing frequency, exercise intolerance worsening
userMessage: Longer warm-up reduces exercise-induced bronchospasm. Swimming is often best tolerated. Keep inhaler accessible.
notes: Longer warm-up reduces EIB. Swimming often best tolerated.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Peripheral Artery Disease
referOutIf: Rest pain, non-healing wounds, sudden onset cold/pale limb
userMessage: Walk until moderate claudication pain, rest until gone, repeat. This is the gold standard treatment.
notes: Claudication-based walking program is gold standard.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Pulmonary Fibrosis
referOutIf: SpO2 dropping below 88% during exercise, increasing dyspnea, declining function
userMessage: Supplemental oxygen may be needed during exercise. Monitor SpO2.
notes: Monitor SpO2. Supplemental O2 may be needed.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Lymphedema
referOutIf: Sudden increase in swelling, signs of infection (cellulitis), skin breakdown
userMessage: Exercise with compression garments. Resistance training does NOT worsen lymphedema.
notes: Exercise with compression. Resistance training is safe (evidence-based).

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Type 2 Diabetes
referOutIf: Hypoglycemia not resolving, autonomic neuropathy symptoms, proliferative retinopathy
userMessage: Exercise improves insulin sensitivity. Monitor glucose before/after. Carry fast-acting carbs. Post-meal walking is especially effective.
notes: Post-meal walking extremely effective. Monitor blood glucose.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Type 1 Diabetes
referOutIf: Recurrent severe hypoglycemia, DKA symptoms, autonomic neuropathy
userMessage: More complex glucose management than T2. Adjust insulin and carbs based on exercise type, duration, and intensity.
notes: Adjust insulin and carbs based on exercise type/duration/intensity.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Thyroid Disorders (Hypo/Hyper)
referOutIf: Thyroid storm signs, severe fatigue despite treatment, cardiac symptoms
userMessage: Hypothyroid: exercise helps fatigue and weight management. Hyperthyroid: avoid overexertion and monitor heart rate.
notes: Hypothyroid: exercise helps. Hyperthyroid: avoid overexertion, monitor HR.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Obesity
avoid: Floor exercises if getting up/down is difficult
referOutIf: Chest pain, severe joint pain, severe shortness of breath
userMessage: Start with what's comfortable. Aquatic exercise is excellent. Non-weight-bearing options available. Progressive overload.
notes: Start comfortable. Aquatic exercise excellent. Progressive overload.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Metabolic Syndrome
referOutIf: Uncontrolled BP, chest pain, severe glucose instability
userMessage: Exercise addresses all components: blood pressure, glucose, waist circumference, and lipids. Circuit training is particularly effective.
notes: Exercise addresses all metabolic syndrome components.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Depression
referOutIf: Suicidal ideation, severe functional impairment, not responding to treatment
userMessage: Strong evidence that regular exercise improves mood and function in depression. Exercise is a powerful adjunct to treatment — we'll build consistency with activities you actually enjoy.
notes: Removed 'as effective as antidepressants' claim. Evidence supports exercise as effective adjunct with strong effect sizes for mood improvement. Behavioral activation principles apply — start small, build consistency.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Anxiety
referOutIf: Panic attacks worsening, agoraphobia preventing exercise, severe avoidance
userMessage: Exercise reduces state and trait anxiety. Breathing exercises for acute episodes. Regular exercise for prevention.
notes: Reduces state and trait anxiety. Breathing exercises for acute.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: PTSD
referOutIf: Dissociation during exercise, worsening symptoms, severe avoidance
userMessage: Trauma-informed approach. Body awareness exercises. Sense of control and empowerment through training.
notes: Trauma-informed approach. Body awareness. Control and empowerment.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: ADHD
referOutIf: Exercise not helping, medication interaction concerns
userMessage: Exercise improves executive function, attention, and mood in ADHD. Morning exercise before work/school is optimal. Complex movements engage focus.
notes: Improves executive function. Morning exercise optimal. Complex movements engage focus.
source: ADHD exercise research, Ratey J. Spark

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Insomnia
avoid: High intensity exercise within 2 hours of bedtime
referOutIf: Chronic insomnia not responding, sleep apnea suspected, daytime impairment severe
userMessage: Regular exercise improves sleep quality. Morning/afternoon preferred. Avoid intense evening workouts.
notes: Regular exercise improves sleep quality. Avoid intense evening workouts.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Pregnancy — 1st Trimester
referOutIf: Bleeding, severe nausea preventing hydration, doctor restricts exercise
userMessage: Can maintain pre-pregnancy exercise intensity if previously active. Hydrate. Avoid overheating.
notes: Can maintain pre-pregnancy intensity if previously active.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Pregnancy — 2nd/3rd Trimester
referOutIf: Bleeding, contractions, fluid leaking, decreased fetal movement, chest pain, headache
userMessage: No lying flat on back after 16 weeks. Modify all supine exercises to incline or side-lying.
notes: No supine after 16 weeks (vena cava compression). Diastasis recti screening.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Postpartum
avoid: High impact before pelvic floor recovery
referOutIf: Pelvic organ prolapse symptoms, persistent diastasis, urinary incontinence worsening
userMessage: 6-week clearance from OB. Pelvic floor FIRST, then core, then load.
notes: 6-week clearance. PF first, core second, load third.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Diastasis Recti
referOutIf: Gap not closing after 8 weeks, visible coning persisting, herniation suspected
userMessage: Check for coning during exercises. If midline bulges, modify or regress. Pelvic floor + TVA co-activation is the foundation.
notes: Check for coning. PF + TVA co-activation foundation.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Pelvic Floor Dysfunction
avoid: Heavy bearing down without PF engagement
referOutIf: Organ prolapse symptoms, persistent incontinence, pelvic pain not improving
userMessage: Coordinate pelvic floor with breathing. Exhale = engage PF on exertion.
notes: Screen for hypertonic vs hypotonic PF — approach differs.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Sarcopenia (Age-Related Muscle Loss)
referOutIf: Rapid functional decline, falls, fractures
userMessage: Resistance training is the #1 intervention. Progressive overload essential. Protein 1.2-1.6g/kg/day.
notes: Resistance training #1 intervention. Protein 1.2-1.6g/kg/day.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Falls Risk / Balance Disorder
referOutIf: Recurrent falls, new neurological signs, syncope
userMessage: Tai chi reduces falls 20-40%. Balance training 3x/week. Strengthen hips and ankles.
notes: Tai chi reduces falls 20-40%. Balance training 3x/week.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Lower Limb Amputation
referOutIf: Residual limb wound issues, phantom pain uncontrolled, prosthetic fit problems
userMessage: Prevent overuse of intact limb. Upper body and core strength are priorities. Cardiovascular fitness is important.
notes: Prevent overuse of intact limb. Phantom pain may benefit from mirror therapy.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Upper Limb Amputation
referOutIf: Residual limb issues, overuse injuries in remaining limb
userMessage: Full lower body training. Adapt upper body exercises. Machine-based may be easier than free weights.
notes: Adapt exercises. Unilateral training. Machine-based may be easier.

severity_description.5: Severe, needs medical clearance before exercise

name: Total Shoulder Replacement (Anatomic TSA)

severity_description.5: Severe, needs clearance

modifications.liftingLimit: No lifting >10lbs for 6 weeks, >25lbs for 12 weeks

referOutIf: New instability, sudden loss of motion, significant pain increase, signs of infection, component loosening symptoms
userMessage: Anatomic TSA preserves normal shoulder mechanics. We progress through passive → active-assist → active → resisted phases. Lifting limits are strict early on.
notes: Anatomic TSA: cuff must be intact. Different from reverse TSA. Lifting limits and push-up restrictions per surgeon.

name: Reverse Total Shoulder Replacement

severity_description.5: Severe, needs clearance

referOutIf: Dislocation (combined adduction + IR position), sudden weakness, instability, infection signs
userMessage: Reverse TSA relies on your deltoid instead of rotator cuff. Push-ups and dips are permanently restricted. We build deltoid strength safely.
notes: Reverse TSA: deltoid-dependent arm. No push-ups permanently. Dislocation risk with combined adduction + internal rotation.

name: Rotator Cuff Repair (Post-Op)

severity_description.5: Severe, needs clearance

phases.phase1_passive: Weeks 0-6: Passive ROM only. Pendulums, pulley, table slides. No active shoulder movement.
phases.phase2_activeAssist: Weeks 6-12: Active-assist ROM. Wand exercises, wall slides. Begin scapular exercises.
phases.phase3_active: Weeks 12-16: Active ROM. Begin band ER, prone Y/T/W. Light resistance.
phases.phase4_resisted: Weeks 16+: Progressive resistance. Tear size and tissue quality affect timeline.

referOutIf: Sudden loss of active ROM (re-tear concern), significant pain increase, new weakness, signs of infection
userMessage: Rotator cuff repair follows strict phases: passive → active-assist → active → resisted. Band ER and prone exercises wait until Phase 3. Tear size affects your timeline.
notes: Explicit phase progression. Small tears progress faster than massive tears. Tissue quality (fatty infiltration) affects healing potential. Don't allow band ER or prone Y/T/W before Phase 3.
source: AAOS rotator cuff repair rehabilitation guidelines

name: Dupuytren's Contracture

severity_description.5: Severe

referOutIf: Rapid progression, tabletop test positive (can't flatten hand), functional limitation in daily activities
userMessage: Dupuytren's is managed differently from trigger finger. Gentle ROM and grip work are appropriate. Forceful stretching can aggravate the condition.
notes: Different from trigger finger. Grip strengthening IS appropriate here (unlike trigger finger). Refer if progressing rapidly or functional limitation.
source: Hand therapy guidelines for Dupuytren's contracture

name: Type 2 Diabetes
avoid: Exercise if blood glucose <100 or >250 mg/dL
referOutIf: Blood glucose <70 (hypoglycemia) or >250 (hyperglycemia), signs of ketoacidosis (nausea, vomiting, fruity breath), new foot wounds, vision changes, chest pain during exercise
userMessage: Exercise is powerful medicine for type 2 diabetes. We check blood glucose before every session and modify intensity based on your numbers. Always check your feet before and after exercise.
notes: Glucose safety: don't exercise if BG <100 or >250. Add foot checks before/after exercise. Screen for peripheral neuropathy (affects balance, sensation). Retinopathy limits heavy Valsalva and high-intensity exercise.
source: ADA Standards of Care, ACSM Exercise and Type 2 Diabetes guidelines

name: Type 1 Diabetes

severity_description.5: Frequent hypo/hyperglycemia

avoid: Exercise if blood glucose <100 without carb plan
Exercise if blood glucose >250 with ketones
referOutIf: Blood glucose <70 with symptoms, ketones present, blood glucose >300, signs of DKA, unexplained hypoglycemia during/after exercise, new foot wounds
userMessage: Type 1 diabetes requires glucose and ketone monitoring before exercise. We plan carbohydrate intake around your sessions and adjust based on your insulin timing.
notes: CGM/SMBG checks mandatory before exercise. No exercise if ketones present. Carbohydrate planning essential. Insulin-exercise interactions are complex — reduce basal for prolonged aerobic, may need carbs for anaerobic. Hypoglycemia risk continues 24+ hours post-exercise.
source: ADA Standards of Care, Riddell et al. Lancet Diabetes 2017

name: Lumbar Microdiscectomy (Post-Op)
avoid: Conventional deadlifts and barbell back squats (axial compression)
Russian twists and cable woodchops (loaded rotation)
Good mornings and back extension machine
Any exercise combining flexion + rotation under load

modifications.coreExercises: Anti-movement ONLY (dead bug, bird dog, side plank after week 4)
modifications.liftingRestriction: No lifting >10-15 lbs for first 6 weeks

phases.weeks_0_2: Walking + light stretching only. No bending, twisting, or lifting >10-15 lbs.
phases.weeks_2_6: Add core stability (dead bug, bird dog), gentle ROM. Gradually increase lifting limit.
phases.weeks_6_8: Surgeon clearance for full activity. Light resistance training. Stationary bike.
phases.weeks_8_12: McKenzie press-ups if extension-biased. Progressive loading with neutral spine.
phases.months_3_6: Return to heavier lifting with proper form. Swimming allowed. Running with clearance.
phases.months_6_plus: Most can return to pre-surgical activity levels. Maintain core stability program.

referOutIf: New radiating pain, progressive numbness/weakness in legs, bowel/bladder changes, increased pain with any exercise, wound changes
userMessage: Your microdiscectomy recovery follows a careful progression. We protect your spine while rebuilding core stability. Most people return to full activity by 3-6 months.
source: Ohio State lumbar discectomy protocol, Mass General rehabilitation guidelines, Spine-Health return to exercise guidelines

name: Patellofemoral Pain Syndrome (Runner's Knee)
avoid: Deep squats with knee valgus (knee caving inward)
Full-depth leg press with heavy load
Open kinetic chain leg extension through pain

modifications.squatDepth: Monitor form, limit depth if painful. Knee must track over 2nd toe.
modifications.hipStrengthening: Critical — hip abductors and external rotators address root cause.
modifications.runningModification: Reduce mileage, avoid hills, consider gait analysis

phases.weeks_0_4: Hip strengthening focus (clamshells, side-lying abduction, monster walks). VMO activation. Patellar taping if helpful.
phases.weeks_4_8: Progressive loading. Squats with form correction. Single-leg exercises introduced.
phases.weeks_8_12: Return to running with gait modification. Sport-specific training.

referOutIf: Locking, giving way, significant swelling, pain not improving after 6 weeks of targeted hip/quad strengthening
userMessage: Patellofemoral pain responds well to exercise — especially hip strengthening. We focus on your hip abductors and external rotators because weak hips are the most common cause of knee tracking problems.
source: JOSPT 2019 Clinical Practice Guidelines (APTA), Mass General PFPS protocol

name: Finger Pulley Strain (Grade I — Mild)
avoid: Dynamic climbing moves on small holds

modifications.allGripExercises: Open hand grip ONLY — no half crimp, no full crimp
modifications.hangboard: Jug holds only at 50% perceived max for first 4 weeks
modifications.climbing: Reduce grade by 3-4 levels, no overhangs, no small holds, H-tape affected finger
modifications.pullingExercises: Use lifting straps for rows, pulldowns to reduce grip demand

phases.weeks_1_2: Unload: H-taping during any activity, gentle ROM, tendon glides 4-5x daily, finger extensor work, NO climbing or hangboard
phases.weeks_3_4: Mobility + light loading: isometric palm crimps (pain-free only), putty squeezes, continue tendon glides, light climbing on jugs only with tape
phases.weeks_5_8: Progressive loading: farmer crimps with light weight progressing weekly, open hand hangboard (jug holds), climbing 3-4 grades below max with tape
phases.weeks_9_12: Return to training: gradual reintroduction of half-crimp on large edges, progressive hangboard loading, climbing approaching normal grade with tape

progressionCriteria: Pain-free at current level for 2 full weeks before advancing. Any pain = stay at current level or regress.
referOutIf: Pain not improving after 3 weeks, visible bowstringing, inability to make a fist, swelling not resolving, numbness or tingling in finger
userMessage: Your finger pulley needs protection while it heals. Grade I strains heal well with proper management — 88% of climbers return to their previous level (Schneeberger & Schweizer). We're modifying your training to keep you active while the pulley strengthens.
notes: Most commonly A2 or A4 pulley. Pain with crimping but no bowstringing. Middle and ring fingers most vulnerable (Vigouroux 2008).
source: Cooper & LaStayo 2019, Vagy 2023 Rock Rehab Pyramid, Schneeberger & Schweizer (47 pulley ruptures study)

severity_description.1: Mild tenderness at pulley, full grip strength, pain only under load
severity_description.2: Moderate tenderness, slight grip weakness, pain with crimping activities

name: Finger Pulley Strain (Grade II — Moderate)

modifications.allPullingExercises: Use lifting straps to remove grip demand for 4-8 weeks
modifications.upperBody: Focus on pushing exercises (antagonist work) which is beneficial during recovery
modifications.lowerBody: Full training allowed — legs don't stress finger pulleys

phases.weeks_1_4: Unload: H-taping or pulley ring orthosis (Hartnett 2024 protocol), ROM exercises only, NO grip loading, push and leg exercises encouraged
phases.weeks_5_8: Light loading: palm crimps → putty crimps progression (pain-free only), begin open hand jug hangs (feet on ground, 30% bodyweight)
phases.weeks_9_12: Progressive loading: farmer crimps with weight, open hand hangboard bodyweight, light climbing (5+ grades below max) with tape
phases.weeks_13_16: Return to training: gradual reintroduction of half-crimp, progressive hangboard, climbing approaching normal

progressionCriteria: Pain-free at current level for 2 full weeks before advancing.
referOutIf: Bowstringing present, pain not improving by week 6, inability to progress through loading phases
userMessage: Moderate pulley strain needs 12-16 weeks of managed rehabilitation. Your training continues — we're shifting to pushing and leg work while your finger heals. Most climbers fully recover from Grade II strains.
source: Lutter et al. 2021 (BJSM), Hartnett 2024 (Journal of Hand Therapy), Vagy Rock Rehab Pyramid

severity_description.3: Significant tenderness, mild swelling, pain with daily gripping, heard a pop during activity

name: Finger Pulley Rupture (Grade III-IV — Severe)
avoid: ALL grip loading for 8 weeks minimum

modifications.allExercises: No grip demand whatsoever — use straps, machines, push/leg focus
modifications.dailyLife: Taping or splinting as directed by hand specialist

phases.weeks_1_4: Medical management: follow hand specialist protocol, gentle ROM only, taping/splinting
phases.weeks_5_8: Begin gentle loading under PT guidance: isometric palm presses, putty work
phases.weeks_9_16: Progressive loading per PT protocol: gradual grip reintroduction
phases.weeks_16_plus: Return to climbing under PT clearance only

referOutIf: IMMEDIATELY — bowstringing visible, complete inability to flex finger, multiple pulley involvement may require surgical consultation
userMessage: This is a serious finger injury that needs medical evaluation. We've removed ALL grip-loading exercises from your plan. Your training continues with pushing, legs, cardio, and core. See a hand specialist or climbing-specialized PT within the first week.

severity_description.4: Complete single pulley rupture, bowstringing may be present, significant swelling
severity_description.5: Multiple pulley involvement, obvious deformity, requires surgical evaluation

name: Finger Flexor Tendinopathy (Climber's Finger)

modifications.hangboard: Reduce to 60-70% intensity, open hand only, increase rest between sets to 5 min
modifications.climbing: Reduce volume 40%, avoid crimping, focus on technique over power
modifications.pullingExercises: Reduce grip demand — use straps for deadlifts, rows

phases.weeks_1_3: Reduce load: drop hangboard to 60% max, no small edges, isometric holds 30-40% max 5×10s daily, tendon glides, eccentric wrist curls
phases.weeks_4_6: Rebuild: progressive isometric loading on hangboard (increase 5% per week), introduce eccentric finger exercises, maintain climbing at reduced volume
phases.weeks_7_12: Return to full: gradual return to normal hangboard intensity, reintroduce half-crimp on moderate edges, climbing volume returns to normal

progressionCriteria: Morning stiffness resolving, pain during activity decreasing, grip strength improving on testing.
referOutIf: Pain worsening despite load management after 4 weeks, loss of finger ROM, swelling developing
userMessage: Tendinopathy means your tendons are irritated from overuse — not injured. The treatment is controlled loading, not complete rest. We're adjusting your grip intensity while keeping you climbing and building tendon resilience.
notes: Gradual onset, worse with gripping, morning stiffness. Eccentric loading stimulates tendon remodeling (GRSM 2025 tendon rehab principles).
source: GRSM 2025 (8 tendon rehab principles), Vagy 2023, Union PT 2025

severity_description.1: Mild morning stiffness, pain only during high-load gripping
severity_description.2: Morning stiffness lasting 30+ min, pain during moderate gripping
severity_description.3: Stiffness throughout day, pain with daily gripping activities, grip weakness

name: Finger Tenosynovitis (Tendon Sheath Inflammation)

modifications.gripType: Open hand only — crimping compresses the sheath

phases.weeks_1_3: Reduce volume 50%, open hand grips only, tendon glides 4x daily, contrast therapy (warm 3min / cold 1min × 3 cycles)
phases.weeks_4_6: Gradual volume increase if catching has resolved, continue tendon glides, monitor for recurrence
phases.weeks_6_plus: Return to normal if symptom-free for 2 weeks

referOutIf: Finger locks and won't straighten, catching worsening after 4 weeks, pain at rest, multiple fingers affected
userMessage: Your tendon sheath is inflamed — the 'catching' sensation is the swollen tendon getting stuck. We're reducing volume and grip intensity while adding exercises to calm the inflammation. This typically resolves in 4-6 weeks with proper management.
source: Schöffl 2015 (climbing injury epidemiology), hand surgery clinical guidelines

severity_description.1: Occasional catching, no pain, resolves quickly
severity_description.2: Frequent catching, mild pain, morning stiffness
severity_description.3: Catching with pain, locking episodes, difficulty making a fist
